ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general miso soup class (FOODON_03315671); no specific clam-and-salmon miso soup subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0EJW covers miso soup generically. No specific clam-and-salmon variant code exists; closest parent match documented.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for clam and salmon miso soup; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

allergens: gluten (miso paste may contain barley or wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: One small bowl of clams and salmon miso soup weighing approximately 250 g, typically served as a side dish alongside sushi, bento, or a Japanese set meal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Miso soup, with tofu and seaweed (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods); supplemented with USDA entries for cooked clams and cooked salmon for protein and fat adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry exists for clam-and-salmon miso soup. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central miso soup entries (FDC ID 172451 — Soup, miso, prepared with water) combined with proportional adjustments for added clam and salmon content. Input record values (energy 60 kcal, protein 6.0 g, fat 2.4 g, carbohydrate 3.2 g, sodium 340 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water, and several vitamins are set to -1 where USDA data for this composite dish is not directly available as a single composite entry.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Miso soup analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for clam-and-salmon miso soup. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the analogous food 'miso soup' from the University of Sydney GI Database and supporting peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). The soup has very low carbohydrate content (3.2 g/100 g), predominantly from miso paste and trace seaweed; protein and fat from clams and salmon further blunt glycaemic response. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (40 × 3.2 × 2.5) / 100 = 3.2 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (340 mg/100 g) from miso paste and dashi stock
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a bowl (≤125 g) and request reduced-miso or low-sodium dashi preparation to keep total sodium intake from this dish below 425 mg per serving; daily sodium target for hypertensive adults is ≤1500–2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.2 g/100 g), low GI (~40), and high protein (6.0 g/100 g) supporting stable postprandial glucose
type2_diabetes.guidance: Clams and salmon miso soup is suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its low carbohydrate load, low glycemic index, and high-quality protein from seafood; avoid adding rice or noodles to the soup, which would substantially raise the glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from salmon offset by elevated sodium (340 mg/100 g) from miso and dashi
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Salmon provides cardioprotective EPA and DHA omega-3 fatty acids and the dish is low in saturated fat (0.6 g/100 g); however, the high sodium content warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease — request low-sodium preparation and limit to one small bowl per meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) from salmon with anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Salmon in this soup provides omega-3 fatty acids that reduce joint inflammation; miso paste is high in sodium and excessive intake may worsen tissue swelling, so reduced-miso preparation is recommended for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Clams and Salmon Miso Soup (あさりとサーモンの味噌汁, Asari to Sāmon no Misoshiru) is a Japanese-style savoury soup combining tender steamed clams and flaked or sliced salmon pieces in a warm miso broth made from fermented soybean paste (miso) dissolved in dashi stock. The soup typically also contains silken tofu cubes and wakame seaweed, giving it a delicate umami depth, a slightly salty and earthy flavour, and a light, brothy texture. It is commonly served as a side dish in Japanese sushi restaurants, bento meal sets, and teishoku (set meal) contexts, including in Singapore's Japanese dining scene. Nutritionally, per 100 g the soup provides approximately 60 kcal, 6.0 g protein, 2.4 g fat (0.6 g saturated), 3.2 g carbohydrate, 1.2 g sugar, 0.4 g dietary fibre, and 340 mg sodium. The high-quality protein from clams and salmon, combined with omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) from salmon, makes this soup nutritionally beneficial for joint health and cardiovascular protection. However, the sodium content of 340 mg/100 g — driven by miso paste and dashi — is a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management in type 2 diabetes. Allergens include fish, shellfish, and soy; miso paste may also contain gluten from barley or wheat.
common_user_queries: Is miso soup good for diabetes?
Is miso soup high in sodium?
Clam miso soup calories per bowl
Is miso soup suitable for hypertension?
あさりとサーモンの味噌汁 カロリー
meal_context: Light starter or side at Japanese restaurants in Singapore

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for clam-and-salmon miso soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central miso soup entries with proportional adjustments for clam and salmon content. Input record values are consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for miso soup. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated), full micronutrient panel (vitamins A, B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, folate, D, E), mineral panel (potassium, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium), and moisture data for this composite clam-and-salmon miso soup dish are not available as a single composite entry in USDA FoodData Central (consulted April 2026) or the Singapore HPB database; disaggregated per-ingredient data exists but cannot be reliably combined without introducing unacceptable estimation error for these micronutrients. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible/trace for this preparation per USDA miso soup reference FDC ID 172451). The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a primary nutrient evidence source as it does not qualify as an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; USDA FoodData Central was used instead. The dish is served in Singapore's Japanese restaurant context; country_of_origin in the input was listed as Singapore but cuisine origin is Japan. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 subclass exists for clam-and-salmon miso soup.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'; ensured all -1 values in per_100g were documented in notes field.

Iteration 3: Expanded notes field to explicitly document the missing-data basis for every -1 field (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g), stating the reason data is unavailable and citing USDA FoodData Central as the source consulted that confirmed unavailability, thereby resolving the g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate failure from iteration 2 validator feedback.
